future_opportunities: Develop richer patient world models for additional cohorts, interventions, and longitudinal clinical prediction tasks.

title: Drive-JEPA: Video JEPA Meets Multimodal Trajectory Distillation for End-to-End Driving
authors: Linhan Wang; Zichong Yang; Chen Bai; Guoxiang Zhang; Xiaotong Liu; Xiaoyin Zheng; Xiao-Xiao Long; Chang-Tien Lu; Cheng Lu
principal_argument: Combining V-JEPA pretraining with multimodal trajectory distillation can better align end-to-end driving representations with planning and behavioral ambiguity.
methodology: Adapts V-JEPA to large-scale driving videos, then adds a proposal-centric planner that distills simulator-generated and human trajectories with momentum-aware selection.
main_findings: On NAVSIM, the V-JEPA representation plus a simple decoder beats prior work in the perception-free setting, and the full Drive-JEPA system sets a new state of the art.
limitations_or_gaps: The summary reports benchmark results on NAVSIM, but does not discuss broader real-world deployment or other driving benchmarks.
future_opportunities: Extend predictive video pretraining and multimodal trajectory distillation to broader autonomous-driving planning systems.

title: Hierarchical JEPA Meets Predictive Remote Control in Beyond 5G Networks
authors: Abanoub M. Girgis; Ibtissam Labriji; Mehdi Bennis
principal_argument: Hierarchical JEPA can improve scalable predictive remote control by operating directly in compact embedding space under bandwidth limits.
methodology: Proposes a three-level hierarchical predictor over low-dimensional embeddings for different temporal resolutions, with control actions derived in latent space rather than reconstructed state space.
main_findings: In inverted cart-pole simulations, H-JEPA supports up to 42.83% more devices under limited wireless capacity without sacrificing control performance.
limitations_or_gaps: The summary only reports simulation results on inverted cart-pole systems, leaving broader system validation open.
future_opportunities: Evaluate hierarchical JEPA control in more complex wireless networked control scenarios and with richer device observations.

title: WirelessJEPA: A Multi-Antenna Foundation Model using Spatio-temporal Wireless Latent Predictions
authors: Viet Chu; Omar Mashaal; Hatem Abou-Zeid
principal_argument: JEPA can serve as a generalizable wireless foundation model when adapted to real-world multi-antenna IQ data with structured spatiotemporal latent prediction.
methodology: The paper reshapes multi-antenna IQ streams into a 2D antenna-time representation, applies block masking and sparse convolutional processing, and trains WirelessJEPA to predict latent representations of masked signal regions with spatiotemporal mask geometries before evaluating six downstream tasks.
main_findings: WirelessJEPA learns general-purpose wireless representations without handcrafted contrastive augmentations and shows robust performance and strong task generalization across six downstream tasks.
limitations_or_gaps: The abstract does not provide per-task quantitative detail, failure cases, or evaluation beyond the six reported downstream tasks.
future_opportunities: Scale JEPA-style pretraining to broader wireless settings, test additional downstream tasks and modalities, and refine antenna-time masking strategies.

title: A Latent Space Framework for Modeling Transient Engine Emissions Using Joint Embedding Predictive Architectures
authors: Ganesh Sundaram; Tobias Gehra; Jonas Ulmen; Mirjan Heubaum; Daniel Görges; Michael Günthner
principal_argument: A structured JEPA latent space can model transient engine emissions more accurately and efficiently than monolithic neural baselines.
methodology: Learns emission dynamics from combined PEMS and high-frequency hardware-in-the-loop data using a JEPA framework, then supports pruning and post-training quantization for deployment.
main_findings: The approach outperforms strong LSTM baselines in data efficiency, predictive accuracy, and generalization, while quantization and pruning greatly reduce footprint with negligible loss.
limitations_or_gaps: The summary is specific to transient engine-emission modeling and does not describe results beyond the reported datasets and regimes.
future_opportunities: Use the efficient JEPA model for onboard emission control, model predictive control, or model-based reinforcement learning in future powertrains.

title: Spatiotemporal Semantic V2X Framework for Cooperative Collision Prediction
authors: Murat Arda Onsu; Poonam Lohan; Burak Kantarci; Aisha Syed; Matthew Andrews; Sean Kennedy
principal_argument: Semantic V2X communication based on V-JEPA embeddings can support cooperative collision prediction while dramatically lowering communication load relative to raw video transmission.
methodology: RSU-mounted cameras generate spatiotemporal semantic embeddings of future frames with V-JEPA in a digital twin traffic environment, and vehicles use a lightweight attentive probe and classifier to decode those embeddings for imminent collision prediction.
main_findings: The framework improves collision-prediction F1 by 10% and cuts transmission requirements by four orders of magnitude versus sending raw video, while preserving predictive utility.
limitations_or_gaps: The abstract centers on a digital twin evaluation and does not describe real-world deployment performance, robustness, or detailed latency trade-offs.
future_opportunities: Validate semantic V2X collision prediction in live ITS settings, optimize embedding processing and communication pipelines, and extend the approach to other cooperative safety tasks.

title: RadJEPA: Radiology Encoder for Chest X-Rays via Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture
authors: Anas Anwarul Haq Khan; Mariam Husain; Kshitij Jadhav
principal_argument: Robust radiology image encoders can be learned from unlabeled chest X-rays without language supervision by predicting masked-region latent representations.
methodology: Introduces RadJEPA, a self-supervised JEPA framework pretrained solely on unlabeled chest X-rays and evaluated on disease classification, semantic segmentation, and report generation.
main_findings: RadJEPA outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, including Rad-DINO, across the reported radiology benchmarks.
limitations_or_gaps: The summary centers on chest X-rays, so extension to other medical imaging modalities is not established here.
future_opportunities: Apply language-free JEPA pretraining to broader radiology and medical-imaging representation-learning tasks.

title: VJEPA: Variational Joint Embedding Predictive Architectures as Probabilistic World Models
authors: Yongchao Huang
principal_argument: Deterministic JEPA objectives obscure probabilistic semantics and limit use in stochastic control, so a variational probabilistic JEPA can provide uncertainty-aware world modeling without reconstructing observations.
methodology: Introduces Variational JEPA to learn a predictive distribution over future latent states with a variational objective, develops Bayesian JEPA with a product-of-experts belief factorization, proves links to PSRs/Bayesian filtering and control sufficiency, and tests the approach in a noisy environment experiment.
main_findings: The paper claims VJEPA unifies JEPA-style representation learning with predictive state representations and Bayesian filtering, can avoid collapse, and in noisy experiments filters nuisance distractors better than generative baselines while enabling uncertainty estimates and modular constraint handling.
limitations_or_gaps: Evidence described in the abstract is centered on theory plus a noisy-environment experiment; broader empirical validation across more control domains is not reported there.
future_opportunities: Apply probabilistic JEPA world models to broader uncertainty-aware planning problems and further explore zero-shot transfer or modular constraint satisfaction with the Bayesian extension.

title: Action100M: A Large-scale Video Action Dataset
authors: Delong Chen; Tejaswi Kasarla; Yejin Bang; Mustafa Shukor; Willy Chung; Jade Yu; Allen Bolourchi; Theo Moutakanni; Pascale Fung
principal_argument: A very large, automatically constructed open-vocabulary video action dataset can provide a stronger foundation for scalable video understanding and world modeling.
methodology: Action100M is built from 1.2M instructional videos using V-JEPA 2 embeddings for hierarchical temporal segmentation, a Tree-of-Captions pipeline, and GPT-OSS-120B with multi-round Self-Refine to produce structured action annotations; VL-JEPA is then trained on the dataset.
main_findings: The pipeline yields O(100 million) temporally localized segments, and training VL-JEPA on Action100M produces consistent data-scaling gains and strong zero-shot action-recognition performance across benchmarks.
limitations_or_gaps: The abstract does not report annotation error rates, dataset biases, or a detailed breakdown of where automated labeling is strongest or weakest.
future_opportunities: Use Action100M for larger-scale pretraining and world-model research, improve automated annotation quality, and study scaling behavior across more video tasks.

title: Video Joint-Embedding Predictive Architectures for Facial Expression Recognition
authors: Lennart Eing; Cristina Luna-Jiménez; Silvan Mertes; Elisabeth André
principal_argument: V-JEPA pretraining can support facial expression recognition by learning semantically useful video embeddings without relying on pixel-level reconstruction.
methodology: Uses a pre-trained V-JEPA video encoder and trains shallow classifiers on the RAVDESS and CREMA-D datasets, with additional cross-dataset evaluation.
main_findings: The abstract reports state-of-the-art performance on RAVDESS, the best vision-based result on CREMA-D (+1.48 WAR), and strong cross-dataset generalization.
limitations_or_gaps: The evidence presented is limited to the RAVDESS and CREMA-D FER benchmarks with shallow classifiers; wider task coverage is not discussed in the abstract.
future_opportunities: Extend embedding-based JEPA pretraining to broader FER benchmarks and related affective video understanding tasks.

title: Akasha 2: Hamiltonian State Space Duality and Visual-Language Joint Embedding Predictive Architectur
authors: Yani Meziani
principal_argument: Combining Hamiltonian State Space Duality with VL-JEPA and physics-inspired inductive biases can yield a multimodal latent world model with stronger spatiotemporal coherence and efficiency.
methodology: Akasha 2 combines a Mamba-3 selective state space model, a Sparse Mixture of Hamiltonian Experts with symplectic integration, Hamiltonian Flow Matching, persistent 3D Gaussian Splatting, and a holographic memory architecture for multimodal modeling and visual synthesis.
main_findings: The abstract reports state-of-the-art video prediction, sub-50 ms mobile latency, 4x faster visual synthesis than diffusion models, 3-18x inference speedups over transformer baselines, and maintained energy conservation over long horizons.
limitations_or_gaps: The metadata gives broad headline claims but not the detailed evaluation settings, ablations, or boundaries of the reported gains.
future_opportunities: Stress-test the physics-inspired design across more multimodal tasks, reproduce the efficiency claims in broader settings, and isolate which inductive biases contribute most.

title: PhysVideoGenerator: Towards Physically Aware Video Generation via Latent Physics Guidance
authors: Siddarth Nilol Kundur Satish; Devesh Jaiswal; Hongyu Chen; Abhishek Bakshi
principal_argument: Injecting V-JEPA 2-derived physics guidance into video diffusion can make physics-aware generation technically feasible.
methodology: The framework trains a lightweight predictor network to regress physical features from noisy diffusion latents, then injects those predicted physics tokens into the temporal attention layers of a DiT-based generator through cross-attention while validating joint-training stability.
main_findings: The abstract shows that diffusion latents retain enough information to recover V-JEPA 2 physical representations and that the joint optimization remains stable, establishing a basis for future physics-aware generative modeling.
limitations_or_gaps: This is presented as a proof of concept focused on architectural feasibility and training stability rather than large-scale gains in perceptual quality or physical realism.
future_opportunities: Run broader evaluations of physical realism, scale the approach to larger generators and datasets, and test stronger physics-guidance mechanisms.

title: HanoiWorld : A Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture BasedWorld Model for Autonomous Vehicle Controller
authors: Tran Tien Dat; Nguyen Hai An; Nguyen Khanh Viet Dung; Nguyen Duy Duc
principal_argument: A JEPA-based world model may offer a more safety-aware and effective alternative to data-hungry pixel-reconstruction reinforcement learning for autonomous vehicle control.
methodology: Introduces Hanoi-World, a JEPA-based world model with an RNN for long-term horizontal planning, and evaluates it in multiple Highway-Env simulation settings.
main_findings: The abstract says Hanoi-World can produce effective driving plans with safety awareness and achieves a favorable collision rate relative to stated baselines in the simulated environments.
limitations_or_gaps: The reported evidence is confined to Highway-Env simulations, and the abstract does not provide broader validation, detailed metrics, or real-world testing.
future_opportunities: Investigate JEPA-based planning for richer driving scenarios and study whether the safety-oriented world-model approach transfers beyond simulation.

title: BERT-JEPA: Reorganizing CLS Embeddings for Language-Invariant Semantics
authors: Taj Gillin; Adam Lalani; Kenneth Zhang; Marcel Mateos Salles
principal_argument: Adding a JEPA objective to BERT-style models can reorganize the CLS embedding space into more language-invariant semantic structure and improve multilingual performance.
methodology: Proposes BERT-JEPA, which augments BERT-style training with a JEPA objective targeting the CLS embedding space, and evaluates the result on multilingual benchmarks.
main_findings: The abstract states that the approach combats collapsed CLS embeddings, yields a language-agnostic embedding space, and improves results across multilingual benchmarks.
limitations_or_gaps: The summary does not specify the multilingual benchmarks or discuss behavior beyond BERT-style models.
future_opportunities: Explore JEPA-based objectives for multilingual representation learning in other language-model architectures and settings.

title: What Drives Success in Physical Planning with Joint-Embedding Predictive World Models?
authors: Basile Terver; Tsung-Yen Yang; Jean Ponce; Adrien Bardes; Yann LeCun
principal_argument: Success in representation-space physical planning with JEPA world models depends on specific technical choices in architecture, training objective, and planning algorithm.
methodology: The paper defines this family as JEPA-WMs, performs a comparative study of key design components on simulated environments and real robotic data, and combines the strongest choices into a new model.
main_findings: The resulting model outperforms DINO-WM and V-JEPA-2-AC on both navigation and manipulation tasks.
limitations_or_gaps: The abstract does not detail the full quantitative effect of each design choice or whether the conclusions transfer beyond the studied tasks and datasets.
future_opportunities: Extend JEPA-WM analysis to broader robotic settings, probe interactions among planning and representation choices, and test generalization to more unseen tasks and environments.

title: Value-guided action planning with JEPA world models
authors: Matthieu Destrade; Oumayma Bounou; Quentin Le Lidec; Jean Ponce; Yann LeCun
principal_argument: JEPA world models can support better action planning when their embedding geometry is explicitly shaped to reflect goal-conditioned value.
methodology: Introduces a training constraint so the negative goal-conditioned value function for reaching cost is approximated by distance between state embeddings, then compares the method with standard JEPA models on simple control tasks.
main_findings: The abstract reports significantly improved planning performance over standard JEPA models on the evaluated simple control tasks.
limitations_or_gaps: The empirical evidence mentioned is limited to simple control tasks, so broader planning settings are not established by the abstract alone.
future_opportunities: Test value-shaped JEPA representations in more complex control domains and broader model-based planning problems.

title: SLIM-Brain: A Data- and Training-Efficient Foundation Model for fMRI Data Analysis
authors: Mo Wang; Junfeng Xia; Wenhao Ye; Enyu Liu; Kaining Peng; Jianfeng Feng; Quanying Liu; Hongkai Wen
principal_argument: An atlas-free JEPA-style design can improve both data efficiency and training efficiency for voxel-level fMRI foundation modeling without sacrificing fine-grained spatial detail.
methodology: SLIM-Brain uses a two-stage pipeline in which a lightweight temporal extractor ranks windows by saliency and a 4D Hiera-JEPA encoder learns voxel-level representations from the top-k windows while deleting about 70% of masked patches.
main_findings: Across seven public benchmarks, the model achieves new state-of-the-art results while needing only 4 thousand pre-training sessions and roughly 30% of the GPU memory of traditional voxel-level methods.
limitations_or_gaps: The abstract emphasizes benchmark performance and efficiency, but does not spell out cross-site robustness, clinical deployment constraints, or weaker task settings.
future_opportunities: Scale the approach to more neuroimaging cohorts and tasks, refine saliency selection, and test robustness across acquisition settings and populations.

title: KerJEPA: Kernel Discrepancies for Euclidean Self-Supervised Learning
authors: Eric Zimmermann; Harley Wiltzer; Justin Szeto; David Alvarez-Melis; Lester Mackey
principal_argument: Kernel-based regularizers broaden JEPA-style Euclidean self-supervised learning and can improve stability while offering more design flexibility than a single Gaussian-prior regularizer.
methodology: The paper defines a family of KerJEPAs, connects one case to the LeJEPA Epps-Pulley regularizer, expands the set of admissible kernels and priors, and derives the closed-form high-dimensional limit of sliced MMDs.
main_findings: The resulting KerJEPA variants are described as having improved training stability and greater flexibility in regularizer design.
limitations_or_gaps: The abstract does not include benchmark-by-benchmark downstream results or specify which kernels and priors are best in different practical settings.
future_opportunities: Evaluate kernel and prior choices across more tasks, adapt the framework to additional JEPA variants, and study how the theoretical results translate into downstream gains.

title: JEPA-Reasoner: Decoupling Latent Reasoning from Token Generation
authors: Bingyang Kelvin Liu; Ziyu Patrick Chen; David P. Woodruff
principal_argument: Reasoning should be decoupled from token generation so that latent reasoning is less vulnerable to expression errors and can better preserve uncertainty.
methodology: Proposes JEPA-Reasoner, which uses a JEPA module for latent-space reasoning and a separate Talker module for linguistic reconstruction, and evaluates the design in controlled synthetic and natural-language experiments.
main_findings: The abstract claims the decoupled design contains token-level errors, exposes the full latent reasoning trajectory to generation, supports mixed-hypothesis uncertainty, and yields a 149.5% 8-shot GSM8K improvement for a 0.9B model over a coupled Transformer baseline trained on the same data.
limitations_or_gaps: The evidence described is limited to controlled experiments and a specific model scale; wider benchmark coverage is not detailed in the abstract.
future_opportunities: Pursue decoupled latent-reasoning architectures as an alternative scaling path for more robust complex reasoning systems.

title: Seeing Beyond Words: Self-Supervised Visual Learning for Multimodal Large Language Models
authors: Davide Caffagni; Sara Sarto; Marcella Cornia; Lorenzo Baraldi; Pier Luigi Dovesi; Shaghayegh Roohi; Mark Granroth-Wilding; Rita Cucchiara
principal_argument: MLLMs can gain stronger visual understanding by adding JEPA-inspired self-supervised visual learning instead of relying primarily on text supervision.
methodology: The proposed JARVIS framework integrates the I-JEPA learning paradigm into vision-language alignment, uses frozen vision foundation models as context and target encoders, and trains the predictor via early LLM layers.
main_findings: Across different LLM families, JARVIS consistently improves vision-centric benchmark performance without hurting multimodal reasoning ability.
limitations_or_gaps: The abstract does not describe compute trade-offs, where the gains are smallest, or how much the method depends on the chosen frozen vision encoders.
future_opportunities: Apply JEPA-style visual enhancement to broader MLLM settings, study scaling and efficiency, and probe how it changes visual reasoning behavior.

title: Recurrent Video Masked Autoencoders
authors: Daniel Zoran; Nikhil Parthasarathy; Yi Yang; Drew A Hudson; Joao Carreira; Andrew Zisserman
principal_argument: Recurrent computation can make video masked autoencoders more efficient while preserving strong general-purpose video representations.
methodology: RVM combines an asymmetric masking objective with a transformer-based recurrent neural network and trains only with a pixel reconstruction loss to aggregate temporal information efficiently.
main_findings: The model is competitive with VideoMAE and V-JEPA on video-level tasks, matches or exceeds strong image models on geometry-heavy tasks, achieves up to 30x better parameter efficiency in the small-model regime, and supports stable long-horizon feature propagation with linear cost.
limitations_or_gaps: The abstract does not present full trade-offs against all baselines or identify settings where recurrent masking is weaker than attention-heavy alternatives.
future_opportunities: Scale recurrent video representation learning further, test more long-horizon tasks, and explore how recurrence interacts with other self-supervised objectives.

title: VL-JEPA: Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture for Vision-language
authors: Delong Chen; Mustafa Shukor; Theo Moutakanni; Willy Chung; Jade Yu; Tejaswi Kasarla; Yejin Bang; Allen Bolourchi; Yann LeCun; Pascale Fung
principal_argument: Predicting continuous text embeddings rather than autoregressively generating tokens can make vision-language models more parameter-efficient while preserving or improving performance.
methodology: Builds a JEPA-based vision-language model that predicts target text embeddings, uses a lightweight decoder only when text output is needed, introduces selective decoding, and compares against standard token-space training under matched encoder and data conditions.
main_findings: The abstract reports stronger performance than standard token-space training with 50% fewer trainable parameters, 2.85x fewer decoding operations with similar quality under selective decoding, superior average results to CLIP, SigLIP2, and Perception Encoder on video classification and retrieval, and VQA performance comparable to classical VLMs with 1.6B parameters.
limitations_or_gaps: The abstract highlights selected classification, retrieval, and VQA evaluations, but does not detail broader generative-language behavior or other deployment tradeoffs.
future_opportunities: Extend JEPA-style vision-language learning to richer multimodal tasks and further exploit adaptive decoding for efficient inference.

title: JEPA as a Neural Tokenizer: Learning Robust Speech Representations with Density Adaptive Attention
authors: Georgios Ioannides; Christos Constantinou; Aman Chadha; Aaron Elkins; Linsey Pang; Ravid Shwartz-Ziv; Yann LeCun
principal_argument: A JEPA-based latent prediction stage combined with density-adaptive attention can learn robust speech representations that also support efficient neural tokenization.
methodology: Uses a two-stage framework: first JEPA with density adaptive attention learns semantic audio features via masked latent prediction, then finite scalar quantization, mixed-radix packing, and a HiFi-GAN decoder produce reversible tokens and waveform reconstruction.
main_findings: The abstract says the method discovers hierarchical speech structure with adaptive temporal feature selection at 2.5 Hz and yields reversible compressed tokens at 47.5 tokens/sec that are competitive with and often more efficient than existing neural audio codecs.
limitations_or_gaps: The abstract does not name benchmark datasets or downstream speech tasks, so empirical scope is only broadly described there.
future_opportunities: Develop language-model-friendly speech tokenizers and compressed audio representations building on JEPA-style latent speech learning.

title: MIND-V: Hierarchical World Model for Long-Horizon Robotic Manipulation with RL-based Physical Alignment
authors: Ruicheng Zhang; Mingyang Zhang; Jun Zhou; Zhangrui Guo; Zunnan Xu; Xiaofan Liu; Zhizhou Zhong; Puxin Yan; Haocheng Luo; Xiu Li
principal_argument: A hierarchical world model with explicit semantic reasoning and RL-based physical alignment can synthesize more plausible long-horizon robotic manipulation videos and support policy learning.
methodology: MIND-V combines a Semantic Reasoning Hub, a Behavioral Semantic Bridge, a Motor Video Generator, staged visual future rollouts, and GRPO post-training with a Physical Foresight Coherence reward that uses V-JEPA2 as a physics referee.
main_findings: The abstract reports state-of-the-art performance in long-horizon simulation and meaningful value for downstream policy learning while emphasizing physically plausible and logically coherent video synthesis.
limitations_or_gaps: The evidence described in the abstract is mainly simulation-based, with no detailed real-world robotic deployment or component-level quantitative breakdown.
future_opportunities: Test the framework on physical robots, expand autonomous embodied data synthesis, and study how world-model planning and physical alignment interact at larger scales.

title: Opinion: Learning Intuitive Physics May Require More than Visual Data
authors: Ellen Su; Solim Legris; Todd M. Gureckis; Mengye Ren
principal_argument: Improving intuitive-physics learning may require more than changing visual data volume or distribution for current V-JEPA-style models.
methodology: The authors pretrain a V-JEPA model on the developmentally realistic, egocentric SAYCam dataset and evaluate the resulting representations on the IntPhys2 benchmark.
main_findings: Training on SAYCam, despite its developmental realism, does not produce significant gains on IntPhys2, suggesting that data distribution and volume alone are insufficient for current architectures.
limitations_or_gaps: The abstract is limited to one dataset framing and benchmark setting, and leaves open whether different architectures, objectives, or nonvisual signals would change the outcome.
future_opportunities: Investigate multimodal learning, architectural changes, and alternative training objectives for intuitive physics beyond visual-only pretraining.

title: DisMo: Disentangled Motion Representations for Open-World Motion Transfer
authors: Thomas Ressler-Antal; Frank Fundel; Malek Ben Alaya; Stefan Andreas Baumann; Felix Krause; Ming Gui; Björn Ommer
principal_argument: A motion representation disentangled from static content can enable open-world motion transfer and stronger motion understanding than content-entangled video models.
methodology: DisMo learns abstract motion representations directly from raw video through an image-space reconstruction objective and connects them to existing video generators via lightweight adapters.
main_findings: The method supports motion transfer across semantically unrelated entities, improves control without the usual fidelity-versus-prompt trade-off described in prior work, and outperforms V-JEPA on cited zero-shot action-classification benchmarks.
limitations_or_gaps: The abstract does not quantify computational costs, failure cases, or how performance varies across different generator backbones and transfer scenarios.
future_opportunities: Integrate the representation into more video generators, expand motion-editing applications, and probe its value on broader downstream motion benchmarks.

title: Health system learning achieves generalist neuroimaging models
authors: Akhil Kondepudi; Akshay Rao; Chenhui Zhao; Yiwei Lyu; Samir Harake; Soumyanil Banerjee; Rushikesh Joshi; Anna-Katharina Meissner; Renly Hou; Cheng Jiang; Asadur Chowdury; Ashok Srinivasan; Brian Athey; Vikas Gulani; Aditya Pandey; Honglak Lee; Todd Hollon
principal_argument: Training directly on large-scale private clinical neuroimaging data can produce generalist medical vision models that outperform frontier public-data models on neuroimaging tasks.
methodology: The work trains NeuroVFM on 5.24 million clinical MRI and CT volumes using a scalable volumetric joint-embedding predictive architecture and then pairs it with open-source language models through lightweight visual instruction tuning for report generation.
main_findings: NeuroVFM achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple clinical tasks, shows emergent neuroanatomic understanding and interpretable grounding, improves radiology report generation, and reduces hallucinated findings and critical errors relative to frontier models.
limitations_or_gaps: The abstract does not discuss external validation across institutions, data-governance constraints, or operational deployment details beyond the reported task results.
future_opportunities: Extend health system learning across more institutions and imaging modalities, deepen safety and governance studies, and adapt the framework to additional clinical workflows.

title: CrossJEPA: Cross-Modal Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture for Efficient 3D Representation Learning from 2D Images
authors: Avishka Perera; Kumal Hewagamage; Saeedha Nazar; Kavishka Abeywardana; Hasitha Gallella; Ranga Rodrigo; Mohamed Afham
principal_argument: Cross-modal JEPA pretraining can turn 2D image supervision into an efficient route for strong 3D representation learning from point clouds.
methodology: Introduces CrossJEPA, which distills embeddings from a frozen image foundation model into a point-cloud encoder by predicting rendered 2D view embeddings, with cross-domain projection conditioning and cached target embeddings for efficiency.
main_findings: The abstract reports new state-of-the-art linear-probing accuracy on ModelNet40 (94.2%) and ScanObjectNN (88.3%) using 14.1M pretraining parameters and about six hours on a single GPU, while emphasizing memory and compute efficiency.
limitations_or_gaps: The evidence emphasized in the abstract centers on linear probing over two 3D benchmarks; broader 3D tasks are not discussed there.
future_opportunities: Apply cross-modal JEPA distillation to additional 3D tasks and further exploit efficient frozen-teacher pretraining in resource-constrained settings.

title: DSeq-JEPA: Discriminative Sequential Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture
authors: Xiangteng He; Shunsuke Sakai; Shivam Chandhok; Sara Beery; Kun Yuan; Nicolas Padoy; Tatsuhito Hasegawa; Leonid Sigal
principal_argument: Sequentially predicting image regions in discriminative order can overcome the limitations of parallel I-JEPA prediction and yield stronger visual representations.
methodology: Proposes DSeq-JEPA, which uses attention-derived saliency to identify primary discriminative regions and then predicts later regions sequentially in a curriculum-like order, with evaluations across classification, fine-grained recognition, detection, segmentation, and reasoning tasks.
main_findings: The abstract states that DSeq-JEPA consistently learns more discriminative and generalizable representations than I-JEPA variants across the evaluated tasks.
limitations_or_gaps: The summary focuses on comparisons with I-JEPA variants on visual tasks and does not discuss possible computational tradeoffs in the abstract.
future_opportunities: Explore sequential discriminative prediction in broader self-supervised visual learning and reasoning settings.

title: POMA-3D: The Point Map Way to 3D Scene Understanding
authors: Ye Mao; Weixun Luo; Ranran Huang; Junpeng Jing; Krystian Mikolajczyk
principal_argument: Point maps, combined with JEPA-style multi-view consistency, can provide a strong self-supervised foundation for 3D scene understanding using geometric inputs alone.
methodology: POMA-3D learns from point maps laid out on structured 2D grids, transfers 2D priors through view-to-scene alignment, enforces cross-view consistency with POMA-JEPA, and pretrains on the new ScenePoint dataset built from room-level RGB-D scenes and 2D image scenes.
main_findings: The model serves as a strong specialist and generalist 3D backbone across tasks including 3D question answering, embodied navigation, scene retrieval, and embodied localization, all using only 3D coordinates.
limitations_or_gaps: The abstract does not detail trade-offs against RGB-based approaches, compute demands, or which task settings benefit most from point-map pretraining.
future_opportunities: Scale point-map pretraining further, test more embodied and 3D reasoning tasks, and explore richer multimodal extensions built on the POMA-JEPA framework.

title: Beyond Generative AI: World Models for Clinical Prediction, Counterfactuals, and Planning
authors: Mohammad Areeb Qazi; Maryam Nadeem; Mohammad Yaqub
principal_argument: Clinical AI needs predictive, temporally coherent, and causally grounded world models rather than relying only on large generative models for decision support.
methodology: Review paper surveying healthcare world-model work across medical imaging, EHR-based disease progression, and robotic surgery, while introducing a four-level capability rubric from temporal prediction to planning/control.
main_findings: Most reviewed systems reach temporal or action-conditioned prediction (L1-L2), while counterfactual decision support (L3) is less common and planning/control (L4) is rare.
limitations_or_gaps: The review highlights under-specified action spaces and safety constraints, weak interventional validation, incomplete multimodal state construction, and limited trajectory-level uncertainty calibration.
future_opportunities: The paper calls for prediction-first clinical world models that combine generative backbones with causal or mechanical foundations for safer decision support.

title: DINOv3 as a Frozen Encoder for CRPS-Oriented Probabilistic Rainfall Nowcasting
authors: Luciano Araujo Dourado Filho; Almir Moreira da Silva Neto; Anthony Miyaguchi; Rodrigo Pereira David; Rodrigo Tripodi Calumby; Lukáš Picek
principal_argument: A frozen satellite vision encoder paired with a V-JEPA-style projector and lightweight probabilistic head can deliver efficient and competitive probabilistic rainfall nowcasting.
methodology: Attach a video projector and probabilistic head to a pretrained DINOv3-SAT493M encoder, map tokens to a discrete empirical CDF over 4-hour rainfall, optimize with Ranked Probability Score, and compare against 3D-UNET baselines.
main_findings: On Weather4Cast 2025, the method reached a CRPS of 3.5102, reported as about a 26% effectiveness gain over the best 3D-UNET baseline.
limitations_or_gaps: Not specified in summary.
future_opportunities: The summary positions the approach as a promising, computationally efficient path for probabilistic rainfall nowcasting.

title: Koopman Invariants as Drivers of Emergent Time-Series Clustering in Joint-Embedding Predictive Architectures
authors: Pablo Ruiz-Morales; Dries Vanoost; Davy Pissoort; Mathias Verbeke
principal_argument: JEPA time-series clustering can be explained by the predictive objective implicitly learning Koopman invariants, rather than by an unexplained emergent effect.
methodology: Develops a theoretical analysis showing when an idealized JEPA loss is minimized by regime-indicator representations tied to Koopman eigenfunctions, and validates the explanation on synthetic data with known dynamics.
main_findings: The abstract claims the theory explains regime clustering, identifies a near-identity linear predictor as the key inductive bias for learning invariants, and links predictor design to representation disentanglement.
limitations_or_gaps: Validation is described on synthetic data and an idealized theoretical setting, with no real-world time-series evaluation mentioned in the abstract.
future_opportunities: Use the Koopman-based perspective to design more interpretable and robust JEPA models for time-series analysis.

title: PriVi: Towards A General-Purpose Video Model For Primate Behavior In The Wild
authors: Felix B. Mueller; Jan F. Meier; Timo Lueddecke; Richard Vogg; Roger L. Freixanet; Valentin Hassler; Tiffany Bosshard; Elif Karakoc; William J. O'Hearn; Sofia M. Pereira; Sandro Sehner; Kaja Wierucka; Judith Burkart; Claudia Fichtel; Julia Fischer; Alexander Gail; Catherine Hobaiter; Julia Ostner; Liran Samuni; Oliver Schülke; Neda Shahidi; Erin G. Wessling; Alexander S. Ecker
principal_argument: A primate-centric, data-centric pretraining strategy can yield more general and label-efficient video representations for primate behavior analysis than human-centric pretrained models.
methodology: Assemble a 424-hour curated primate video dataset from research and web footage, continue pretraining V-JEPA on it, and evaluate the frozen representations with a lightweight classifier on four benchmark datasets.
main_findings: The approach consistently outperforms prior work, including fully finetuned baselines, scales well with fewer labels, and provides evidence that domain-level pretraining works for video models.
limitations_or_gaps: The summary frames prior methods as limited in generalization, but does not state explicit limitations of PriVi itself.
future_opportunities: The results suggest a promising path for low-label video applications and broader primate behavior research.

title: LeJEPA: Provable and Scalable Self-Supervised Learning Without the Heuristics
authors: Randall Balestriero; Yann LeCun
principal_argument: JEPA training can be simplified and stabilized by enforcing a theoretically optimal isotropic Gaussian embedding distribution, eliminating many common self-supervised heuristics.
methodology: Develop a theory of JEPA embeddings, introduce Sketched Isotropic Gaussian Regularization (SIGReg), combine it with the JEPA predictive loss as LeJEPA, and validate across more than 10 datasets and 60 architectures.
main_findings: LeJEPA is presented as linear in time and memory, stable across hyperparameters and architectures, free of stop-gradient or teacher-student heuristics, and reaches 79% linear-eval accuracy with a ViT-H/14 pretrained on ImageNet-1k.
limitations_or_gaps: The summary emphasizes solving the lack of theory and heuristic-heavy practice in prior JEPAs, but does not describe explicit limitations of LeJEPA.
future_opportunities: The paper aims to provide a theory-friendly, simple ecosystem that could renew interest in self-supervised pretraining as a core AI research direction.

title: Grounding Foundational Vision Models with 3D Human Poses for Robust Action Recognition
authors: Nicholas Babey; Tiffany Gu; Yiheng Li; Cristian Meo; Kevin Zhu
principal_argument: Action recognition becomes more robust when predictive video representations are grounded with explicit 3D human pose information in physical space.
methodology: Fuse V-JEPA 2 representations with CoMotion human pose data and evaluate the combined model on InHARD and UCF-19-Y-OCC against three baselines.
main_findings: The fused model outperforms the baselines, with especially strong gains in complex and highly occluded scenes.
limitations_or_gaps: The summary critiques RGB-only action recognition as superficial, but does not state explicit limitations of the proposed model.
future_opportunities: The findings motivate action-recognition systems that rely more on spatial grounding than on surface-level pattern matching.

title: Foley Control: Aligning a Frozen Latent Text-to-Audio Model to Video
authors: Ciara Rowles; Varun Jampani; Simon Donné; Shimon Vainer; Julian Parker; Zach Evans
principal_argument: Video-guided Foley can be achieved efficiently by keeping pretrained video and text-to-audio models frozen and learning only a small cross-attention bridge between them.
methodology: Condition a frozen Stable Audio Open DiT text-to-audio model on V-JEPA2 video embeddings through compact cross-attention inserted after text cross-attention, with pooled video tokens for lower memory use.
main_findings: Foley Control achieves competitive temporal and semantic alignment with far fewer trainable parameters than recent multimodal systems while preserving prompt controllability and modularity.
limitations_or_gaps: The summary focuses on Video-to-Foley and does not detail broader limitations of the approach.
future_opportunities: The bridge design is presented as extendable to other audio modalities, including speech.

title: Improving the Physics of Video Generation with VJEPA-2 Reward Signal
authors: Jianhao Yuan; Xiaofeng Zhang; Felix Friedrich; Nicolas Beltran-Velez; Melissa Hall; Reyhane Askari-Hemmat; Xiaochuang Han; Nicolas Ballas; Michal Drozdzal; Adriana Romero-Soriano
principal_argument: Self-supervised video world models can supply a reward signal that improves the physical plausibility of generated videos.
methodology: Build on the MAGI-1 video generator and guide generation with VJEPA-2 as a reward signal in a short technical report tied to the PhysicsIQ Challenge.
main_findings: Using VJEPA-2 as reward improves the physics plausibility of the state-of-the-art video generator by about 6%.
limitations_or_gaps: As a short technical report, the summary gives limited detail and does not spell out broader limitations.
future_opportunities: The work suggests further use of SSL-based video world models to make generative video models more physics-aware.

title: Cosmos-Surg-dVRK: World Foundation Model-based Automated Online Evaluation of Surgical Robot Policy Learning
authors: Lukas Zbinden; Nigel Nelson; Juo-Tung Chen; Xinhao Chen; Ji Woong Kim; Mahdi Azizian; Axel Krieger; Sean Huver
principal_argument: A surgical world foundation model can enable automated online evaluation of robot policy learning, reducing dependence on costly and variable physical-robot testing.
methodology: Finetune the Cosmos world foundation model for surgical settings, pair it with a trained video classifier, and evaluate the pipeline on tabletop suture pad and ex-vivo porcine cholecystectomy datasets against real dVRK outcomes and human labels.
main_findings: The automated pipeline shows strong correlation with real dVRK policy outcomes on suture pad tasks, good agreement between human labelers and the V-JEPA 2-derived classifier, and promising early alignment on ex-vivo cholecystectomy tasks.
limitations_or_gaps: Results on more complex surgical procedures are described as preliminary, and the broader real-world evaluation problem remains costly and challenging.
future_opportunities: The platform is positioned as a path toward evaluating increasingly complex surgical procedures in simulation.

title: BADAS: Context Aware Collision Prediction Using Real-World Dashcam Data
authors: Roni Goldshmidt; Hamish Scott; Lorenzo Niccolini; Shizhan Zhu; Daniel Moura; Orly Zvitia
principal_argument: Collision prediction should focus on ego-vehicle threat context, and a V-JEPA2-based model trained on ego-centric dashcam data can reduce false alerts and improve prediction quality.
methodology: Create the BADAS family by re-annotating benchmarks for ego involvement and alert timing, synthesizing negatives, training V-JEPA2-based models end-to-end, and evaluating on DAD, DADA-2000, DoTA, and Nexar.
main_findings: BADAS achieves state-of-the-art AP/AUC, beats a forward-collision ADAS baseline, and produces more realistic time-to-accident estimates; the authors also release an open model and benchmark re-annotations.
limitations_or_gaps: The summary does not describe explicit limitations of BADAS itself, focusing instead on shortcomings of prior collision prediction methods.
future_opportunities: Released weights, code, and re-annotations are intended to support further ego-centric collision prediction research.

title: Gaussian Embeddings: How JEPAs Secretly Learn Your Data Density
authors: Randall Balestriero; Nicolas Ballas; Mike Rabbat; Yann LeCun
principal_argument: The anti-collapse term in JEPAs does more than prevent collapse: it implicitly estimates data density, enabling probability estimates from trained JEPA models.
methodology: Provide a theoretical analysis showing how sample probabilities can be computed in closed form from the model Jacobian, and validate the claim empirically across synthetic data, controlled settings, ImageNet, several JEPA-family methods, and multimodal models.
main_findings: Any successfully trained JEPA can, in principle, yield efficient sample probabilities for density estimation; the authors package this use case as JEPA-SCORE and validate it across datasets and model families.
limitations_or_gaps: Not specified in summary.
future_opportunities: The method is positioned for data curation, outlier detection, and density estimation.

title: EmbodiSwap for Zero-Shot Robot Imitation Learning
authors: Eadom Dessalene; Pavan Mantripragada; Michael Maynord; Yiannis Aloimonos
principal_argument: Photorealistic synthetic robot overlays on human egocentric video, combined with a V-JEPA visual backbone, can support strong zero-shot robot imitation learning.
methodology: Generate synthetic robot-overlay datasets from human videos and robot URDFs, train a closed-loop manipulation policy on the resulting data, and compare V-JEPA with other visual backbones in real-world tests.
main_findings: The V-JEPA-based zero-shot model reaches an 82% success rate in real-world tests and outperforms alternative backbones as well as the few-shot-trained pi_0 baselines discussed in the summary.
limitations_or_gaps: Not specified in summary.
future_opportunities: The released synthetic-data pipeline, datasets, and checkpoints are intended to support reproducible research and broader adoption.

title: What Drives Compositional Generalization in Visual Generative Models?
authors: Karim Farid; Rajat Sahay; Yumna Ali Alnaggar; Simon Schrodi; Volker Fischer; Cordelia Schmid; Thomas Brox
principal_argument: Compositional generalization in visual generative models is strongly shaped by the discreteness of the training objective and by how much conditioning reveals constituent concepts during training.
methodology: Run controlled experiments on image and video generation to study design choices affecting compositional generalization, then augment MaskGIT with an auxiliary continuous JEPA-based objective.
main_findings: The study identifies two key drivers of compositional generalization and shows that adding a continuous JEPA-style objective can improve compositional performance in discrete models such as MaskGIT.
limitations_or_gaps: The summary notes that the mechanisms behind compositional generalization are not fully understood, but does not describe explicit limitations of the proposed study.
future_opportunities: The results suggest that combining discrete and continuous objectives is a useful direction for improving compositional generation.

title: Inferring Dynamic Physical Properties from Video Foundation Models
authors: Guanqi Zhan; Xianzheng Ma; Weidi Xie; Andrew Zisserman
principal_argument: Video foundation models can infer dynamic physical properties from temporal visual evidence, but they still trail methods with direct oracle cues.
methodology: Build new synthetic and real evaluation datasets for elasticity, viscosity, and dynamic friction, then compare an oracle vision-based method, prompt-based readouts on DynamiCrafter and V-JEPA-2, and prompting strategies for multimodal LLMs.
main_findings: Generative and self-supervised video foundation models perform similarly overall, both remain behind the oracle method, and multimodal LLMs are currently weaker though prompt design helps.
limitations_or_gaps: The summary explicitly notes that the tested foundation models lag the oracle and that MLLMs are currently inferior.
future_opportunities: The work points to better prompting and improved video-model readouts as opportunities, and releases the dataset, model, and code.

title: JEPA-T: Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture with Text Fusion for Image Generation
authors: Siheng Wan; Zhengtao Yao; Zhengdao Li; Junhao Dong; Yanshu Li; Yikai Li; Linshan Li; Haoyan Xu; Yijiang Li; Zhikang Dong; Huacan Wang; Jifeng Shen
principal_argument: Late fusion plus objective-level text alignment can make a joint-embedding predictive transformer effective for text-conditioned image generation while keeping the backbone general.
methodology: Introduces JEPA-T, which encodes images and captions into discrete tokens, applies a joint-embedding predictive Transformer, adds cross-attention after the predictor for conditional denoising, injects raw text embeddings before the flow-matching loss, and uses iterative denoising at inference.
main_findings: On ImageNet-1K, the abstract reports strong data efficiency, open-vocabulary generalization, and consistent gains over non-fusion and late-fusion baselines.
limitations_or_gaps: The abstract only names ImageNet-1K evaluations and does not describe broader text-to-image benchmarks or qualitative failure modes.
future_opportunities: Extend JEPA-style multimodal token modeling to broader text-to-image generation settings and other conditional generative tasks.

title: Joint Embeddings Go Temporal
authors: Sofiane Ennadir; Siavash Golkar; Leopoldo Sarra
principal_argument: JEPA-style latent prediction can be adapted effectively to time series, offering a general representation learner for both classification and forecasting.
methodology: Introduce Time Series JEPA (TS-JEPA) for time-series representation learning and evaluate it on standard classification and forecasting benchmarks against current baselines.
main_findings: TS-JEPA matches or surpasses state-of-the-art baselines and shows a strong balance of performance across diverse tasks.
limitations_or_gaps: Not specified in summary.
future_opportunities: The paper positions TS-JEPA as groundwork for future time-series foundation models based on joint embeddings.

title: Rethinking JEPA: Compute-Efficient Video SSL with Frozen Teachers
authors: Xianhang Li; Chen Huang; Chun-Liang Li; Eran Malach; Josh Susskind; Vimal Thilak; Etai Littwin
principal_argument: EMA-updated teachers are not necessary for V-JEPA-style video self-supervision; a frozen teacher can provide a simpler and more compute-efficient alternative.
methodology: Introduces SALT, a two-stage scheme that first trains a target encoder by pixel reconstruction under V-JEPA masking, then freezes it and trains a student to predict the teacher latents on masked regions, followed by frozen-backbone benchmarking and scaling analysis.
main_findings: The abstract reports that SALT students outperform V-JEPA 2 encoders under frozen evaluation, achieve better accuracy-FLOPs tradeoffs, and remain strong even when the teacher is small or sub-optimal.
limitations_or_gaps: The evidence presented is framed around frozen-backbone evaluation and the two-stage setup; the abstract does not detail broader downstream or end-to-end finetuning behavior.
future_opportunities: Use frozen-teacher latent prediction for scalable video SSL and rethink compute allocation toward stronger students rather than more expensive teachers.

title: WavJEPA: Semantic learning unlocks robust audio foundation models for raw waveforms
authors: Goksenin Yuksel; Pierre Guetschel; Michael Tangermann; Marcel van Gerven; Kiki van der Heijden
principal_argument: A waveform-based JEPA focused on semantic representation learning can produce strong, efficient, and robust general-purpose audio representations directly from raw waveforms.
methodology: Propose WavJEPA for raw-waveform learning and WavJEPA-Nat as a multi-channel extension trained on simulated naturalistic scenes, then evaluate them across many downstream tasks and noisy or reverberant settings.
main_findings: WavJEPA substantially outperforms state-of-the-art time-domain audio foundation models while using fewer computational resources, and WavJEPA-Nat is highly robust to reverberation and noise.
limitations_or_gaps: The summary focuses on overcoming prior weaknesses of waveform and noisy-environment learning, but does not spell out explicit limitations of WavJEPA itself.
future_opportunities: The results support low-latency, robust time-domain audio foundation models for real-world applications.

title: Temporal vs. Spatial: Comparing DINOv3 and V-JEPA2 Feature Representations for Video Action Analysis
authors: Sai Varun Kodathala; Rakesh Vunnam
principal_argument: Spatial-only and temporally modeled self-supervised video features offer distinct strengths for action analysis, so model choice should depend on task demands and reliability needs.
methodology: Compares DINOv3 and V-JEPA2 on the UCF Sports dataset using classification, clustering, intra-class consistency, inter-class discrimination, and action-specific analyses.
main_findings: DINOv3 delivers better clustering and stronger separation for pose-identifiable actions, while V-JEPA2 yields more even performance across action types with much lower variance.
limitations_or_gaps: The comparison is reported on UCF Sports and centered on two architectures, so generality beyond this dataset and model pair is not established in the summary.
future_opportunities: Match spatial or temporal feature extractors to action type, and test whether hybrid approaches can combine DINOv3 discrimination with V-JEPA2 consistency.

title: Self-supervised learning of imaging and clinical signatures using a multimodal joint-embedding predictive architecture
authors: Thomas Z. Li; Aravind R. Krishnan; Lianrui Zuo; John M. Still; Kim L. Sandler; Fabien Maldonado; Thomas A. Lasko; Bennett A. Landman
principal_argument: Multimodal JEPA pretraining on unlabeled longitudinal CT and EHR data can improve pulmonary nodule diagnosis, but its benefits may not transfer cleanly out of distribution.
methodology: Curates an unlabeled multimodal patient archive for JEPA pretraining, then performs supervised finetuning and compares against unregularized multimodal and imaging-only models on internal and external cohorts, alongside a synthetic environment for analyzing failure cases.
main_findings: The abstract reports improved internal-cohort AUC over both comparison models, but worse external-cohort AUC than the imaging-only model, highlighting both gains and generalization limits.
limitations_or_gaps: A key limitation is explicit in the abstract: the method underperforms on the external cohort, and the study is focused on one medical application and institution-specific unlabeled archive.
future_opportunities: Study when multimodal JEPA pretraining helps or hurts medical prediction and improve robustness to external distribution shifts.

title: Domain-Adaptive Pretraining Improves Primate Behavior Recognition
authors: Felix B. Mueller; Timo Lueddecke; Richard Vogg; Alexander S. Ecker
principal_argument: Domain-adaptive pretraining on unlabeled in-domain video can materially improve primate behavior recognition beyond using a pretrained V-JEPA model alone.
methodology: Applies self-supervised learning with a pretrained V-JEPA backbone, continues pretraining with in-domain data via domain-adaptive pretraining, and evaluates on the PanAf and ChimpACT ape-behavior datasets.
main_findings: The method surpasses published state of the art on both datasets, and the summary states that most of the gain comes from the domain-adaptive pretraining stage.
limitations_or_gaps: The evidence in the summary is limited to two primate datasets and does not describe failure cases, annotation trade-offs, or broader animal behavior settings.
future_opportunities: Extend domain-adaptive pretraining to other species and conservation datasets, especially where unlabeled video is abundant but labels are scarce.

title: Why and How Auxiliary Tasks Improve JEPA Representations
authors: Jiacan Yu; Siyi Chen; Mingrui Liu; Nono Horiuchi; Vladimir Braverman; Zicheng Xu; Dan Haramati; Randall Balestriero
principal_argument: Auxiliary tasks improve JEPA representations because they anchor the distinctions that latent dynamics alone might otherwise collapse.
methodology: Provides a theoretical analysis of a JEPA variant with a jointly trained auxiliary regression head, proves a no-unhealthy-collapse result in deterministic MDPs, and runs controlled ablations in a counting environment.
main_findings: The abstract claims that driving both transition-consistency and auxiliary losses to zero forces non-equivalent observations to remain distinct in latent space, and that joint training with the auxiliary head yields richer representations than separate training.
limitations_or_gaps: The formal result is stated for deterministic MDPs and the experiments described are in a counting environment, so broader stochastic settings are not established in the abstract.
future_opportunities: Design auxiliary objectives that encode the right equivalence relations for stronger JEPA encoders in model-based learning.

title: LLM-JEPA: Large Language Models Meet Joint Embedding Predictive Architectures
authors: Hai Huang; Yann LeCun; Randall Balestriero
principal_argument: JEPA-style embedding-space objectives can improve LLM pretraining and finetuning over standard reconstruction-oriented language training objectives.
methodology: Introduces LLM-JEPA, a JEPA-based training approach for language models, and evaluates it across multiple datasets and model families in both pretraining and finetuning settings.
main_findings: The abstract reports significant gains over standard LLM objectives on NL-RX, GSM8K, Spider, and RottenTomatoes, with robustness to overfitting across Llama3, OpenELM, Gemma2, and Olmo models.
limitations_or_gaps: The abstract does not specify detailed architectural trade-offs, compute cost, or settings where the JEPA objective may underperform.
future_opportunities: The work opens a path for broader JEPA-style training research for language models across additional tasks, scales, and training regimes.

title: Leveraging Generic Foundation Models for Multimodal Surgical Data Analysis
authors: Simon Pezold; Jérôme A. Kurylec; Jan S. Liechti; Beat P. Müller; Joël L. Lavanchy
principal_argument: Generic video foundation models become more useful for surgical data science when adapted with unlabeled domain video and paired with complementary operating-room data streams.
methodology: Uses V-JEPA as the video foundation of a multimodal model, tests pretrained and domain-finetuned variants, and adds separate encoders for additional time-resolved OR modalities across surgery prediction and phase-recognition tasks.
main_findings: Domain-specific finetuning improves performance, multimodal integration helps on the in-house liver surgery data, and the pretrained video-only baseline is already competitive on HeiCo surgical phase recognition.
limitations_or_gaps: Results are tied to the described liver surgery and HeiCo settings, and the summary does not specify how well the approach transfers across procedures, hospitals, or modality configurations.
future_opportunities: Broaden evaluation across surgical workflows and explore which additional OR streams are most complementary to adapted V-JEPA embeddings.

title: Learning State-Space Models of Dynamic Systems from Arbitrary Data using Joint Embedding Predictive Architectures
authors: Jonas Ulmen; Ganesh Sundaram; Daniel Görges
principal_argument: JEPA-style representation learning combined with continuous-time dynamics can recover structured latent state-space models from arbitrary observations.
methodology: Combines sequence embeddings with neural ODEs and uses losses enforcing contractive embeddings and Lipschitz-constrained state transitions; demonstrates the approach on a simple pendulum from image observations.
main_findings: The method is reported to generate structured latent state-space models for a pendulum using only image data, showing promise for world modeling from arbitrary observations.
limitations_or_gaps: Evidence in the abstract is limited to a simple pendulum example, with no detail on more complex systems, robustness, or downstream control performance.
future_opportunities: The abstract points to more general control and estimation methods in robotics built on these learned latent dynamics.

title: Elucidating the Role of Feature Normalization in IJEPA
authors: Adam Colton
principal_argument: Layer normalization at the IJEPA teacher output harms useful token-energy structure, and replacing it with DynTanh improves representation learning.
methodology: Analyzes the effect of teacher-side feature normalization in IJEPA, replaces layer normalization with DynTanh, inspects loss-map behavior, and evaluates on ImageNet linear probing and NYU Depth V2 depth estimation.
main_findings: Feature layer normalization is associated with checkerboard artifacts and flattened token energies, while DynTanh preserves longer-tailed loss behavior and improves both ImageNet probe accuracy and depth RMSE.
limitations_or_gaps: The summary focuses on IJEPA and two reported downstream evaluations, so broader architectural applicability and sensitivity to other settings are not detailed.
future_opportunities: Test energy-preserving target transformations in other JEPA-style models and study when token-energy structure matters most for downstream tasks.

title: Speaking in Words, Thinking in Logic: A Dual-Process Framework in QA Systems
authors: Tuan Bui; Trong Le; Phat Thai; Sang Nguyen; Minh Hua; Ngan Pham; Thang Bui; Tho Quan
principal_argument: A lightweight Text-JEPA plus symbolic solver pipeline can provide more efficient and interpretable closed-domain QA than relying only on large LLM-based systems.
methodology: Introduces Text-JEPA for natural-language-to-first-order-logic conversion, couples it with the Z3 solver for reasoning, and evaluates the pipeline with custom conversion, reasoning, and rank-correlation metrics on domain-specific datasets.
main_findings: The framework achieves competitive performance with lower computational overhead and supports transparent reasoning through structured logic representations.
limitations_or_gaps: The summary emphasizes closed-domain datasets and custom metrics, and does not establish behavior for open-domain QA or more diverse reasoning settings.
future_opportunities: Apply the dual-process design to more specialized domains and refine logic-conversion methods that preserve reasoning quality at low computational cost.

title: Predicting Brain Responses To Natural Movies With Multimodal LLMs
authors: Cesar Kadir Torrico Villanueva; Jiaxin Cindy Tu; Mihir Tripathy; Connor Lane; Rishab Iyer; Paul S. Scotti
principal_argument: Ensembling temporally aligned multimodal foundation-model features can improve prediction of brain responses to natural movie stimuli.
methodology: Extracts representations from video, speech, text, vision-text, and vision-text-audio models, aligns them to fMRI time series, maps them with a lightweight shared-plus-subject-specific encoder, and ensembles many tuned variants for the Algonauts 2025 challenge.
main_findings: The final system reached a mean Pearson correlation of 0.2085 on withheld out-of-distribution movies, ranking fourth, and the authors report a last-minute optimization that could have placed second.
limitations_or_gaps: This is a challenge-oriented pipeline summary, and it does not disentangle the contribution of each modality or assess performance beyond the described competition setup.
future_opportunities: Study modality-specific contributions and further optimize multimodal ensembles for out-of-distribution neural encoding of movie stimuli.

title: Self-Supervised Ultrasound-Video Segmentation with Feature Prediction and 3D Localised Loss
authors: Edward Ellis; Robert Mendel; Andrew Bulpitt; Nasim Parsa; Michael F Byrne; Sharib Ali
principal_argument: V-JEPA is a promising self-supervised pretraining framework for ultrasound video, and adding a 3D localisation auxiliary task improves downstream segmentation.
methodology: Adapts V-JEPA to ultrasound video data, adds a 3D localisation auxiliary objective to strengthen locality in ViT representations, and evaluates frozen-encoder segmentation under varying label availability.
main_findings: The proposed auxiliary task improves segmentation performance across frozen encoder setups, with gains reported both at full data and at 10 percent of the training data.
limitations_or_gaps: The summary frames the work around ultrasound video segmentation and frozen-encoder experiments, without evidence here about other imaging modalities or model families.
future_opportunities: Extend JEPA-style ultrasound pretraining to additional medical video tasks and study whether locality-focused auxiliary losses help in other low-data clinical settings.

title: VIBE: Video-Input Brain Encoder for fMRI Response Modeling
authors: Daniel Carlström Schad; Shrey Dixit; Janis Keck; Viktor Studenyak; Aleksandr Shpilevoi; Andrej Bicanski
principal_argument: Fusing multimodal movie features with a two-stage transformer can improve fMRI response modeling for both in-distribution and out-of-distribution stimuli.
methodology: Builds a modality-fusion transformer and a temporally decoding prediction transformer over video, audio, and text features from open-source models, trained on 65 hours of CNeuroMod movie data and ensembled across 20 seeds.
main_findings: VIBE attains mean parcel-wise Pearson correlations of 0.3225 in-distribution and 0.2125 out-of-distribution, and an earlier version won Phase-1 and placed second overall in the Algonauts 2025 challenge.
limitations_or_gaps: The summary reports aggregate challenge-style metrics but does not isolate which modalities or architectural pieces are most responsible for performance.
future_opportunities: Probe modality importance, simplify the ensemble-heavy setup, and test the architecture on broader neural response prediction datasets.

title: Improving Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture with Diffusion Noise
authors: Yuping Qiu; Rui Zhu; Ying-cong Chen
principal_argument: Injecting diffusion noise into JEPA-style masked image modeling can strengthen self-supervised visual representation learning.
methodology: Proposes N-JEPA, which incorporates diffusion noise through masked-token position embeddings and adds a multi-level noise schedule as a feature-augmentation strategy.
main_findings: A comprehensive downstream classification study is said to confirm the effectiveness of the method and the robustness benefits of the noise schedule.
limitations_or_gaps: The abstract focuses on recognition performance and does not report detailed benchmark outcomes, generative behavior, or failure modes.
future_opportunities: The work suggests deeper exploration of the connection between diffusion-style corruption and self-supervised recognition models, alongside public code release.

title: From Video to EEG: Adapting Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture to Uncover Saptiotemporal Dynamics in Brain Signal Analysis
authors: Amirabbas Hojjati; Lu Li; Ibrahim Hameed; Anis Yazidi; Pedro G. Lind; Rabindra Khadka
principal_argument: Treating EEG as video-like sequences allows V-JEPA to learn scalable and interpretable spatiotemporal EEG representations for classification.
methodology: Adapts V-JEPA to EEG classification with joint embeddings and adaptive masking, and evaluates the model on the TUH Abnormal EEG dataset while examining learned signal patterns.
main_findings: EEG-VJEPA reportedly outperforms existing state-of-the-art EEG classifiers and captures physiologically relevant spatial and temporal patterns that support interpretability.
limitations_or_gaps: The abstract highlights results on one public dataset and does not describe performance across broader EEG tasks, sites, or deployment conditions.
future_opportunities: The paper positions EEG-VJEPA as a candidate framework for scalable, trustworthy EEG analysis and human-AI support in diagnostic workflows.

title: Development and Comparative Evaluation of Three Artificial Intelligence Models (NLP, LLM, JEPA) for Predicting Triage in Emergency Departments: A 7-Month Retrospective Proof-of-Concept
authors: Edouard Lansiaux; Ramy Azzouz; Emmanuel Chazard; Amélie Vromant; Eric Wiel
principal_argument: A comparative triage study suggests LLM-based modeling is more effective than the tested NLP and JEPA alternatives for emergency-department triage prediction.
methodology: Evaluates three AI systems against the FRENCH triage scale and nurse practice using seven months of adult triage data from Roger Salengro Hospital in Lille, France.
main_findings: The LLM-based URGENTIAPARSE achieved the best reported accuracy, AUC-ROC, and hospitalization-prediction performance, outperforming both the NLP and JEPA models as well as nurse triage.
limitations_or_gaps: This is a seven-month retrospective proof-of-concept at a single hospital, and the abstract notes that adoption still depends on addressing limitations and ethical transparency.
future_opportunities: The summary points toward ethically transparent integration of LLM-based support tools into emergency-department workflows.

title: How do Foundation Models Compare to Skeleton-Based Approaches for Gesture Recognition in Human-Robot Interaction?
authors: Stephanie Käs; Anton Burenko; Louis Markert; Onur Alp Culha; Dennis Mack; Timm Linder; Bastian Leibe
principal_argument: General-purpose foundation models can approach strong gesture-recognition performance, but skeleton-based methods still lead on the studied human-robot interaction task.
methodology: Compares V-JEPA, Gemini Flash 2.0, and the skeleton-based HD-GCN on NUGGET, a gesture dataset designed for intralogistics human-robot communication.
main_findings: HD-GCN performs best, V-JEPA comes close when equipped with a simple task-specific head, and Gemini underperforms in zero-shot gesture recognition from textual descriptions alone.
limitations_or_gaps: The comparison is tied to the NUGGET dataset and the tested models, and the summary indicates zero-shot text-only prompting is insufficient for fine gesture discrimination.
future_opportunities: Explore shared multi-task systems built on V-JEPA and develop better input representations for gesture understanding in multimodal foundation models.

title: Audio-JEPA: Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture for Audio Representation Learning
authors: Ludovic Tuncay; Etienne Labbé; Emmanouil Benetos; Thomas Pellegrini
principal_argument: A straightforward JEPA adaptation to audio can learn competitive audio representations without reconstructing raw waveforms.
methodology: Uses a Vision Transformer to predict masked mel-spectrogram patch representations, pretrains on unlabeled AudioSet clips, and evaluates on the X-ARES speech, music, and environmental sound suite.
main_findings: Despite being a simple translation of the original model and using no hyper-parameter tuning, Audio-JEPA is reported to perform comparably to wav2vec 2.0 and data2vec with less than one-fifth of their training data.
limitations_or_gaps: The abstract presents an intentionally simple adaptation and does not detail scaling behavior, domain-specific weaknesses, or stronger audio-specialized variants.
future_opportunities: Release code and checkpoints and build more audio-specific JEPA variants for speech, music, and environmental sound tasks.

title: Cross-Architecture Knowledge Distillation (KD) for Retinal Fundus Image Anomaly Detection on NVIDIA Jetson Nano
authors: Berk Yilmaz; Aniruddh Aiyengar
principal_argument: Cross-architecture knowledge distillation can compress an I-JEPA-pretrained ViT fundus classifier into an edge-deployable CNN while retaining much of its diagnostic behavior.
methodology: Trains an I-JEPA-pretrained ViT teacher for four-class retinal classification, distills it into a CNN student using partitioned cross-attention and group-wise linear projectors plus multi-view robust training, and targets deployment on Jetson Nano.
main_findings: The student is far smaller than the teacher yet achieves 89 percent classification accuracy and retains roughly 93 percent of the teacher performance according to the summary.
limitations_or_gaps: The paper summary focuses on one retinal imaging classification setup and does not report broader clinical validation or deployment behavior outside the stated edge-device context.
future_opportunities: Validate the distillation framework on additional ophthalmic and low-resource medical imaging tasks, and assess real-world edge deployment constraints more fully.

title: Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture for self-supervised pretraining on polymer molecular graphs
authors: Francesco Piccoli; Gabriel Vogel; Jana M. Weber
principal_argument: JEPA-based self-supervised pretraining can improve polymer graph learning when high-quality labels are scarce.
methodology: Applies JEPA self-supervised pretraining to polymer molecular graphs and compares downstream property-prediction performance across multiple datasets under limited-label conditions.
main_findings: The abstract reports downstream improvements across all tested datasets, with especially strong benefits when labeled data is very scarce.
limitations_or_gaps: The summary does not describe architecture choices, pretraining cost, or whether the gains persist when more labeled data is available.
future_opportunities: The work supports further use of JEPA-style graph pretraining for polymer discovery and other low-label materials-learning settings.

title: From Pixels to CSI: Distilling Latent Dynamics For Efficient Wireless Resource Management
authors: Charbel Bou Chaaya; Abanoub M. Girgis; Mehdi Bennis
principal_argument: Coupled JEPAs can jointly model control dynamics and wireless channel dynamics, enabling more efficient wireless resource management without degrading control performance.
methodology: Uses a control JEPA and a wireless JEPA with cross-modal conditioning, then trains deep reinforcement learning for control and a power predictor for favorable scheduling based on latent CSI representations.
main_findings: In synthetic multimodal simulations, the approach reduces transmit power by more than 50 percent while maintaining control performance comparable to baselines that ignore wireless optimization.
limitations_or_gaps: The evidence described in the summary is simulation-based and synthetic, so real-world wireless-control deployment performance remains unverified there.
future_opportunities: Test the coupled-JEPA strategy on real communication-control systems and examine broader multimodal resource-management settings beyond the synthetic setup.

title: HiT-JEPA: A Hierarchical Self-supervised Trajectory Embedding Framework for Similarity Computation
authors: Lihuan Li; Hao Xue; Shuang Ao; Yang Song; Flora Salim
principal_argument: Hierarchical JEPA representations can jointly capture fine-grained and high-level trajectory semantics for better similarity computation.
methodology: Introduces a three-layer hierarchy that learns point-level, intermediate, and high-level urban trajectory representations within one joint-embedding predictive framework.
main_findings: Experiments on multiple real-world datasets are reported to yield richer multi-scale representations and improved trajectory similarity computation.
limitations_or_gaps: The abstract is centered on similarity tasks and does not discuss other trajectory analytics uses or the computational trade-offs of the hierarchical design.
future_opportunities: The framework could be extended to broader urban trajectory analysis settings that benefit from multi-scale movement representations.

title: V-JEPA 2: Self-Supervised Video Models Enable Understanding, Prediction and Planning
authors: Mido Assran; Adrien Bardes; David Fan; Quentin Garrido; Russell Howes; Mojtaba; Komeili; Matthew Muckley; Ammar Rizvi; Claire Roberts; Koustuv Sinha; Artem Zholus; Sergio Arnaud; Abha Gejji; Ada Martin; Francois Robert Hogan; Daniel Dugas; Piotr Bojanowski; Vasil Khalidov; Patrick Labatut; Francisco Massa; Marc Szafraniec; Kapil Krishnakumar; Yong Li; Xiaodong Ma; Sarath Chandar; Franziska Meier; Yann LeCun; Michael Rabbat; Nicolas Ballas
principal_argument: Large-scale self-supervised video JEPA models can support world understanding, anticipation, question answering, and robotic planning from mostly observational data.
methodology: Pretrains V-JEPA 2 on over 1 million hours of internet video and images, aligns it with a large language model for video question answering, and post-trains an action-conditioned variant with limited robot data for planning.
main_findings: The abstract reports strong motion-understanding results, state-of-the-art human action anticipation and several video-QA scores, plus zero-shot pick-and-place planning on Franka arms in two labs.
limitations_or_gaps: The abstract does not detail compute demands, failure modes, or broader robotics generalization, and the planning setup still relies on post-training with robot data.
future_opportunities: The work opens opportunities for self-supervised world models that connect web-scale video learning to physical-world planning and control.

title: Integrating Dynamical Systems Learning with Foundational Models: A Meta-Evolutionary AI Framework for Clinical Trials
authors: Joseph Geraci; Bessi Qorri; Christian Cumbaa; Mike Tsay; Paul Leonczyk; Luca Pani
principal_argument: A meta-evolutionary framework that combines a small-data, interpretable dynamical-systems model with an LLM strategist can improve clinical-trial subgroup discovery and prediction.
methodology: Formalizes NetraAI with contraction mappings, information geometry, evolutionary search, pseudo-temporal embeddings, and long-range memory, then uses an LLM layer to prioritize variables, inject domain knowledge, and assess robustness in case studies.
main_findings: Across schizophrenia, depression, and pancreatic cancer case studies, the framework identifies small high-effect subpopulations and reportedly lifts weak baseline models to near-perfect classifiers using few features.
limitations_or_gaps: The summary presents case studies rather than broad benchmark evidence, and its link to JEPA is conceptual alignment rather than a direct JEPA-based method.
future_opportunities: Assess the framework on more clinical datasets and further study how meta-level LLM guidance can support reliable, explainable cohort discovery.

title: Time to Embed: Unlocking Foundation Models for Time Series with Channel Descriptions
authors: Utsav Dutta; Sina Khoshfetrat Pakazad; Henrik Ohlsson
